14.text: copy for deep-copying the base model state before LoRA injection.

15.text: We need math.sqrt for scaling attention scores. Dividing by the square root of head dimension keeps dot products from growing too large as dimensions increase.

16.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

17.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

19.text: Import the core PyTorch library for tensor operations and autograd.

20.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear, Embedding, and ModuleList that automatically register their parameters for training.

21.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

23.text: Fix both the Python and PyTorch random seeds so results are reproducible across runs.

24.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

29.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

30.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

36.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

37.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

39.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

40.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

49.text: The LoRA wrapper: the key innovation of this lab. Wraps an existing nn.Linear and adds two tiny matrices that learn to steer the original layer's output.

56.text: Takes an existing trained linear layer and wraps it with low-rank adapters.

58.text: Store the original linear layer. Its weight will be frozen during fine-tuning.

59.text: Freeze the base weight so no gradients flow into it during LoRA fine-tuning.

60.text: Read the dimensions from the existing weight matrix.

61.text: lora_A: the down-projection (rank x d_in). Random init with small values to break symmetry. This matrix learns to read important features from the input.

62.text: lora_B: the up-projection (d_out x rank). Zero init is crucial — it makes the adapter a no-op at the start. The model behaves exactly like the base model until training shifts B away from zero.

64.text: Forward pass: base output + adapter output. Two paths, summed.

67.text: The math: W*x + B*A*x. Since B starts at zero, the adapter contributes nothing initially. As B learns non-zero values, the adapter gently steers the output. With rank=2, A is (2, d_in) and B is (d_out, 2) — tiny compared to W's (d_out, d_in).

74.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

75.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

76.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names.

77.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

80.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

86.text: Normalize by root-mean-square to keep activations stable without learned scale parameters.

89.text: Standard causal attention, identical to lab 03.

92.text: Q, K, V, output projections. LoRA will later wrap wq and wv only (the standard LoRA recipe from the original paper).

93.text: Key projection — not wrapped by LoRA in this lab, following the finding that K projections contribute less to adaptation.

94.text: Value projection — will be wrapped by LoRA alongside wq.

95.text: Output projection — not wrapped by LoRA in this lab.

98.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

99.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

100.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

101.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

103.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

104.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

105.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

106.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

108.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

109.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

112.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

115.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

116.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

118.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

119.text: Run the two-layer MLP with ReLU, producing the feed-forward output for this position.

122.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

125.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

126.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

127.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

128.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

130.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

131.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

132.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

136.text: Full model, identical to lab 03 for pre-training.

139.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

140.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

141.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

142.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

143.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

144.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

148.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian (std=0.08) so activations start at a reasonable scale, neither exploding nor vanishing through the layers.

151.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

152.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

153.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

154.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

155.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

156.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

158.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

164.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

165.text: Set the sampling temperature to 0.5 for moderately conservative name generation.

168.text: Generate names from the model. The optional check_fn marks names that match the target style, and quiet suppresses output (used for merge verification).

170.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

172.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

174.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

176.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits. During generation we re-run the full sequence at each step since there is no KV cache.

178.text: Divide the logits by the temperature before softmax to control how peaked the distribution is.

179.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one.

180.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution to introduce controlled diversity.

181.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

183.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

184.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

186.text: Only print when not in quiet mode (merge verification runs silently).

187.text: If a check function is provided, mark matching names with <-- soft so the output is easy to scan visually.

193.text: Shared training step, used for both pre-training and LoRA fine-tuning.

195.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

196.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

197.text: Convert the token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as its input sequence.

198.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

200.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

201.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

203.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

204.text: Backprop. During LoRA fine-tuning, gradients only flow into the unfrozen LoRA parameters.

205.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

207.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

208.text: Update weights. Only LoRA parameters move because base weights are frozen.

219.text: Instantiate the base model that will first be pre-trained on all names. Later, we will freeze these weights and inject LoRA adapters for fine-tuning.

224.text: Train for 1000 steps of standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus. This gives the model a solid foundation before we specialize it with LoRA.

226.text: Pre-training loop: standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus.

227.text: Pick the next name from the shuffled list, wrapping around when we reach the end.

228.text: Run one training step on the current document, returning the loss value.

233.text: Generate some baseline names to see the pre-trained model's behavior before LoRA.

236.text: Save the pre-trained state so we can restore it for each rank in the ablation. Deep copy because LoRA modifies the model in-place.

241.text: Define vowels for the consonant filter.

242.text: The target phonetic style: names using only the soft consonants m, n, r. This is a distributional property of the whole name — unlike filtering by first letter, it cannot be achieved by input conditioning.

243.text: Filter the dataset to names whose consonants are all in {m, n, r}. Requires at least one consonant so we exclude vowel-only strings.

257.text: Check if a generated name qualifies as 'soft' — all its consonants are drawn from {m, n, r}. Used to score the output and annotate printed names.

262.text: Replace only the targeted nn.Linear layers (by name) with LoRALinear wrappers. Defaults to wq and wv, following the original LoRA paper's finding that query and value projections carry the most adaptation signal.

264.text: Walk the module tree looking for layers whose name matches the target list.

265.text: Found a targeted linear layer — wrap it with a LoRA adapter.

266.text: Swap the original linear layer for the LoRA-wrapped version in-place.

268.text: Recurse into child modules to find nested linear layers.

271.text: Merge LoRA: fold the learned adapter weights back into the base weight matrix. After merging, the model is a standard nn.Linear again with zero runtime overhead.

273.text: Walk the module tree looking for LoRA-wrapped layers.

274.text: Found a LoRA layer — merge it back into a plain linear layer.

275.text: Allocate a standard nn.Linear with the same dimensions, ready to receive the merged weight matrix.

280.text: The merge formula: W_new = W_base + B @ A. Mathematically identical to the LoRA forward pass but baked into a single weight matrix — no adapter overhead at inference time.

281.text: Replace the LoRA wrapper with the merged standard linear layer.

283.text: Descend into child modules to merge any deeper LoRA wrappers.

287.text: Reload a fresh copy of the base model to generate comparison samples.

290.text: Pin the random seed so the base and LoRA models sample from the same random sequence for a fair comparison.

291.text: Generate 20 names from the base model with soft-name markers to establish the baseline hit rate.

299.text: Count base model parameters once for the freeze assertion inside the loop.

304.text: Run the full LoRA experiment at rank 1, 2, and 4 to see how adapter size affects the phonetic shift. This is a rank ablation — a standard technique in LoRA research.

310.text: Restore a fresh copy of the pre-trained base model for each rank so experiments are independent.

312.text: Freeze every parameter in the base model. Only the LoRA matrices (injected next) will be trainable.

316.text: Inject LoRA adapters into query and value projections of each transformer layer.

319.text: Recount parameters — total grows slightly because of the LoRA matrices added on top of the frozen base.

320.text: Count only the trainable parameters (the LoRA A and B matrices).

322.text: Safety check: assert that every base parameter is frozen. If this fails, something went wrong with the freeze.

326.text: Walk the model tree to find all LoRA-wrapped layers and display their dimensions.

333.text: Print each adapter's factorization: the full weight W(d_out x d_in) is adapted by tiny matrices A(rank x d_in) and B(d_out x rank). Their product B@A has the same shape as W but uses far fewer parameters.

336.text: Print the total adapter parameter count, its percentage of total model params, and confirm the base model is fully frozen.

340.text: Collect only the LoRA parameters for the optimizer — base weights are excluded.

341.text: Fresh Adam optimizer for fine-tuning, operating only on the tiny LoRA parameter set.

342.text: Fine-tuning loop: 500 steps on the soft-name subset. Same train_step as pre-training, but gradients only flow into the LoRA matrices.

343.text: Cycle through only the soft-consonant names for fine-tuning.

349.text: Generate 20 names from the LoRA-adapted model with the same seed used for the baseline, so results are directly comparable.

352.text: Count how many generated names satisfy the soft-consonant constraint.

357.text: Merge LoRA adapters back into the base weights so the model is a standard nn.Linear again.

359.text: Generate from the merged model silently and verify every name matches the LoRA output exactly — merging is exact, not an approximation.

362.text: After merging, the parameter count should equal the original base model — all LoRA overhead is gone.

373.text: Print the baseline for context: what fraction of names are 'soft' without any LoRA adaptation.

376.text: Summary table header showing the key metrics: adapter size, percentage of total params, soft-name hit rate, and merge verification.

378.text: Print one row per rank. The table shows diminishing returns: even rank 1 (64 params, ~1.5%) achieves a dramatic shift from baseline, while rank 2+ saturates at ~90%.
